methodologyPage.title: Methodology
methodologyPage.intro: GastrAtlas is built on two core values: Accuracy and Authenticity. This page explains what a recipe or history text actually goes through before it's published on the site, and what technical and editorial safeguards support that process.
methodologyPage.workflowBody: Every piece of content is in one of five states: Draft, AI Review, Editor Review, Published, or Archived. Moving content into "Published" requires at least Historian-level permission — enforced independently both in application code and at the database level (Postgres row-level security policies). Publishing authority is deliberately kept separate from content-drafting authority. Other transitions (draft, in review, archived) can be made with Editor permission.
methodologyPage.aiBody: Because the platform's content base is still in an early stage, first drafts of history and summary text are written with AI assistance. This isn't hidden: any text that hasn't yet completed editorial verification carries an explicit note at the end saying so ("This text is a draft pending editorial verification"). AI never publishes content directly — no text can reach "Published" status without passing through the approval gate described above. The same transparency principle applies to images: when an AI-generated image is used, it is explicitly labeled as such.
methodologyPage.sourcesBody: Every recipe's history is grounded in the sources listed at the bottom of the page. Each source is given a reliability rating from 1 to 5 by the editorial team (shown with ● and ○ marks), and this rating is never hidden from the reader. For contested or uncertain-origin dishes (for example, recipes that are a shared heritage across multiple cuisines), the text is deliberately written in a cautious, non-definitive tone.
methodologyPage.rolesBody: GastrAtlas has five user roles: Visitor, User, Editor, Historian, and Admin. The purpose of this separation is to avoid concentrating both content-creation authority and the authority to "approve" a historical claim in the same person — publishing is only possible with Historian or Admin permission.
methodologyPage.commentsBody: Reader comments enter a moderation queue when submitted and aren't publicly visible until approved by an Editor or higher — until then, they remain visible only to their author.
methodologyPage.correctionsBody: There is always room for error in historical accuracy claims. If you notice a mistake, an omission, or a contested point in a recipe or its history, you can let us know via the comments section below that recipe.
